title: ⚠ MAP KEY — these are MODERN political borders
description: All the coloured regions on this map are MODERN country borders (post-1945). They do NOT represent historical empires (Ottoman, British), colonial territories (Saint-Domingue, Dutch East Indies), or pre-modern geography. The borders are shown to orient students to where coffee is produced today. For historical boundaries, consult Episode 1 primary sources (Women's Petition 1674, Royal Proclamation 1675, Topik & Clarence-Smith 2003).

Ethiopia (modern borders) — origin + producer
description: Modern political border (post-1991). ORIGIN of coffee — Kaffa forest highlands. 421,000 tonnes/year, ~4% of global production, 100% Arabica. Wild coffee genetic diversity is irreplaceable for climate adaptation breeding (Davis et al., 2012).

title: Yemen (modern borders) — first trade hub
description: Modern political border (post-1990 unification). First commercial coffee trade hub — Mocha (Al-Mukha) was the original port. Sufi scholars spread coffee through the Ottoman world from here.

title: Brazil (modern borders) — largest producer today
description: Modern political border. 38% of world coffee, 2.6–3.8 M tonnes/year, grown between 600–1,300 m on the SE plateau. Both Arabica and Robusta. History built on enslaved labour until 1888 abolition; 2024 drought pushed prices up 70% (Rabobank).

title: Colombia (modern borders) — 100% Arabica
description: Modern political border. 760,000 tonnes/year, ~8% of world coffee, grown across three Andean ranges at 1,200–2,000 m. Federación Nacional de Cafeteros organizes 540,000 smallholder producers.

title: Vietnam (modern borders) — world's Robusta leader
description: Modern political border. 1.5–2 M tonnes/year, 17.7% of world coffee — went from almost zero in the 1970s to largest Robusta producer. Central Highlands volcanic basalt at 500–800 m.

title: Indonesia / Sumatra (modern borders)
description: Modern political border (post-1945). Part of Indonesia's 668,000 tonnes/year across Sumatra, Java, Sulawesi. Dutch colonial Cultuurstelsel system forced cultivation until 1870.

title: Indonesia / Java + Sulawesi (modern borders)
description: Modern political border. Island-by-island flavor profiles — same species, radically different results based on soil and microclimate.

title: Central America (modern borders) — Guatemala/Honduras/Nicaragua/El Salvador
description: Modern political borders (post-1821 independence from Spain). Highland coffee belt — specialty Arabica at 1,000–2,000 m. Fair-trade and direct-trade movements concentrated here.

title: East Africa (modern borders) — Uganda/Kenya/Tanzania
description: Modern political borders (post-independence 1960s). Highland Arabica at 1,500–2,000 m. Uganda is also Africa's largest Robusta producer. British colonial cultivation systems transitioned to smallholder ownership.
